data.title: Phillips 66 achieved lowest cost per barrel since 2021 in Q2, CEO says
data.description:
Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) closed +0.5% on Friday after reporting better than expected Q2 adjusted earnings and revenues, helped by higher refining margins and lower operating expenses.

Q2 net income surged to $877M, or $2.15/share, from $487M, or $1.18/share, in Q1, adjusted EBITDA more than tripled to $2.5B from $736M, in Q1, and cash flow from operations jumped to $845M from $187M in the previous quarter.

Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) said its Q2 realized margin rose 12.4% to $11.25/bbl from the year-earlier quarter, while turnaround expenses fell 47% to $53M; its crude capacity utilization was 98%, the best quarterly result since 2018, while adjusted earnings from its refining segment jumped ~30% to $392M.

Some analysts flagged concerns about debt, as Phillips 66's (PSX) net debt to capital ratio for Q2 was 41%, compared to rival refiner Valero's 12%.

Phillips 66 (PSX) CEO Mark Lashier told CNBC in an interview that the company achieved its lowest cost per barrel since 2021 during the quarter.

Lashier noted a strong growth trajectory for its midstream business, which activist investor Elliott Management has said should either be sold or spun off; the segment's adjusted EBITDA rose nearly 10% Y/Y to $972M in Q2 following the acquisition of EPIC NGL - which was renamed Coastal Bend - as well as the opening of another gas-processing plant in the Permian Basin.

The two Phillips 66 (PSX) directors that activist investor Elliott got installed after a boardroom battle are "well integrated" among the other members and are aligned with everyone else on expanding shareholder value and are bringing diverse perspectives as the board continuously reexamines its strategy, the CEO said.

data.title: Phillips 66 outlines plan to organically grow Midstream EBITDA to $4.5B by 2027 as refining achieves record utilization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Phillips 66 (PSX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark E. Lashier reported strong financial and operating results, highlighting that "Our refining assets ran at 98% utilization, the highest since 2018. Clean product yield was over 86%, we captured 99% of our market indicator and achieved our lowest adjusted cost per barrel since 2021." Lashier emphasized the integrated business model by noting, "Midstream generated adjusted EBITDA of approximately $1 billion. We are on track to achieve the $4.5 billion annual EBITDA target in Midstream by 2027. Marketing and Specialties reported its strongest quarter since 2022."
  • Lashier also stated, "We returned over $900 million to shareholders this quarter. The resilience of our integrated business model drives results delivering consistent returns to shareholders."
  • The company welcomed three new Board members, each undergoing "a comprehensive multi-day onboarding process with a broad group of our senior leadership team, equipping them to contribute meaningfully and immediately."
  • CFO Kevin J. Mitchell reported, "Second quarter reported earnings were $877 million or $2.15 per share. Adjusted earnings were $973 million or $2.38 per share... We generated $845 million of operating cash flow. Operating cash flow, excluding working capital, was $1.9 billion. We returned $906 million to shareholders including $419 million of share repurchases. Net debt to capital was 41% and reflects the impact of the acquisition of the Coastal Bend assets."

Outlook

  • Mitchell indicated, "In Chemicals, we expect the global O&P utilization rate to be in the mid-90s. In Refining, we expect the worldwide crude utilization rate to be in the low to mid-90s and turnaround expense to be between $50 million and $60 million."
  • The company reduced full year turnaround guidance by $100 million, with new guidance at $400 million to $450 million compared to the previous $500 million to $550 million. Mitchell stated, "We continue to optimize turnarounds and improve performance."

Financial Results

  • Mitchell highlighted, "Total company adjusted earnings increased $1.3 billion to $973 million compared with prior quarter's adjusted loss of $368 million."
  • The quarter included a $239 million pretax impact of accelerated depreciation due to the plan to cease operations at the Los Angeles refinery in Q4.
  • Operating cash flow, excluding working capital, was $1.9 billion, and the company ended the quarter with $1.1 billion in cash.
  • Midstream results increased mainly due to higher volumes following the acquisition of Coastal Bend assets. Marketing and Specialties showed its strongest quarter since 2022 with $660 million in earnings, though Mitchell noted about $100 million of this was a timing benefit offset from Q1.

Q&A

  • Douglas George Blyth Leggate, Wolfe Research: Asked if the company remains committed to the integrated strategy after recent activism. Lashier: "We continue to evaluate a wide range of strategic alternatives... there's no sacred cows. We're not ideological about anything. We're -- well, we are ideological about one thing and that's shareholder value creation."
  • Leggate followed up on debt levels. Mitchell responded, "The $17 billion of debt on a consolidated basis, we feel puts us in a very comfortable spot... we expect to accomplish that through a combination of cash generated from operations as well as proceeds from dispositions."
  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Inquired about refining performance drivers. Executive Vice President of Refining Richard G. Harbison detailed, "Our mission in refining is to run the assets safely and reliably and then drive world-class performance... We've also established a comprehensive reliability program... We've increased our ability to produce gasoline, diesel and jet and swing between those 3 components."
  • Neil Singhvi Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Asked about global refining balances and Chinese export quotas. Brian M. Mandell, EVP of Marketing & Commercial, responded, "Net refinery additions are below for as far out as we forecast, certainly through the end of the decade... we see a very strong margin environment."
  • Several analysts questioned the outlook for renewable fuels and chemicals. Lashier: "Second quarter was particularly problematic when you think about the disruptions that tariffs caused... Our longer-term view is still consistent, you're seeing rationalization in Europe, you're seeing rationalization rumored in Asia."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on strategic alternatives, Midstream structure, and the outlook for weaker segments, indicating a slightly skeptical but constructive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and transparent tone, emphasizing engagement with shareholders and flexibility in strategy. Lashier stated, "We're fully aligned and the shareholders agree that there's significant value in Phillips 66, and we've got to go out and capture that upside."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from defensive regarding activism to more confident and forward-looking, while analysts maintained a focus on strategic direction and profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year turnaround expense was reduced by $100 million compared to the previous quarter.
  • Adjusted earnings swung from a $368 million loss in Q1 to $973 million in Q2, reflecting improved refining utilization and margins.
  • Strategic focus continues on Midstream organic growth and refining cost reductions, with a more optimistic tone on capturing market upside.
  • Analysts continued to focus on Midstream monetization, refining performance, and balance sheet targets, but questions were less confrontational than in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s confidence increased in discussing execution and value creation, highlighting successful integration of new assets and Board alignment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing challenges in the Chemicals segment due to tariffs and market disruptions, with upside expected as rationalization continues in Europe and Asia.
  • Renewable fuels margins remain weak, leading to reduced run rates and ongoing regulatory headwinds. Mandell explained, "We are running at reduced rates. In the second quarter, we ran at reduced rates, and we continue to run at reduced rates."
  • Analysts raised concerns about exposure to Permian volume growth, refining utilization, and the impact of planned asset dispositions.

Final Takeaway

Phillips 66 emphasized robust financial and operating performance in the second quarter, with record refining utilization and progress toward a $4.5 billion Midstream EBITDA target by 2027. Management reiterated its commitment to optimizing operations, disciplined capital allocation, and returning over 50% of net operating cash flow to shareholders, while maintaining flexibility to pursue value-enhancing strategic alternatives backed by strong Board oversight and shareholder engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Velo3D announces 1-for-15 reverse stock split
data.description:
• Velo3D (OTC:VLDX) has approved a 1-for-15 reverse stock split of the company's common stock, par value $0.00001 per share.
  • The common stock is expected to begin trading on the OTCQX on July 28, 2025, on a split-adjusted basis.
  • The reverse stock split is primarily intended to prepare for the potential listing of the company's common stock on a national securities exchange, the company added.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Actuate Therapeutics registers 1.33M shares for resale by investors
data.description:
• Actuate Therapeutics (NASDAQ:ACTU) filed a prospectus for resale of 1.33m common shares by existing investors. The offering includes 666,497 outstanding shares and 666,497 shares issuable upon warrant exercise.
  • Actuate will not receive proceeds from the resale of shares, but will receive funds from any warrant exercises.
  • Source: SEC filing

data.title: Powell resigning is 'least costly option' to keep Fed independence: El-Arian
data.description:
• Allianz Chief Economic Advisor Mohamed El-Arian doubled down on comments he made Tuesday that Fed Chairman Jerome Powell should resign by saying Friday him doing so would be the "least costly option" in maintaining the Fed's independence.
  • "He should leave because he is the magnet into a growing number of investigations that could lead to it losing its independence," El-Arian, the former CEO of Pimco, told Scott Wapner during an interview of CNBC's "Closing Bell."
  • When asked if Powell has made mistakes during his tenure, El-Arian cited his handling of transitory inflation, adding he was late in hiking interest rates. He also said that Powell's communications haven't been great given that when he does press conferences, the market is more volatile than whan any prior Fed chair has done them.
  • Responding to another question on who should replace Powell, El-Arian replied, "We don't lack good candidates with lots of experience."
  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Wall Street closed the week on a high note as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq set fresh records
data.description:
Wall Street ended higher on Friday, capping off a strong week that saw both the Nasdaq Composite and S&P 500 finish the week at fresh record highs.

At the close of trading the blue-chip Dow (DJI) had advanced by 0.4%, and it once again came close to tagging a new all-time high. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had rounded out the day higher by 0.4%, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) had gained 0.2% on the session. 

For the week the Dow rose 1.2%, the S&P 500 moved up 1.4%, and the Nasdaq Composite popped up 1%.

“Considering that the market this week just got on with the job of making investors quietly richer, there was an awful lot of anxiety and hand-wringing on the part of said investors. There seems to be something about continuous new highs that unnerves people, for what reason we know not.  Do people prefer misery?" Seeking Alpha analyst Alex King said.

"Heading into next week, just as last week, all analysis says a pullback is due.  But so far, any such correction is nowhere to be seen. The pain trade remains …. Up,” King added.

From a sector point of view, nine of the S&P 500’s 11 segments closed in the green, led by strong performances in Materials and Industrials. On the flip side, Energy was the weakest performer of the day as oil (CL1:COM) fell by 1.7%. 

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were relatively muted as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 1 basis point to 4.38%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was near even at 3.91%.

Looking out at the economic calendar, durable goods dropped less than feared in June, with new orders for manufactured durable goods: -9.3% M/M to $311.8B vs. -10.4% consensus and +16.5% prior (revised from +16.4%).

“As it stands, hard economic data in the US has shown signs of weakness over the past couple of quarters. But, contrary to the belief of many, the US economy remains robust and far from recessionary levels,” Seeking Alpha analyst Christopher Yates stated.

On the earnings end, Intel (INTC) shares dropped 8.4% on Friday after the company's bottom line in the second quarter missed Wall Street estimates. The firm's Q3 outlook also failed to impress investors. The company also announced a 15% workforce reduction.

data.title: GameSquare regains Nasdaq compliance
data.description:
• GameSquare (NASDAQ:GAME) announced on Friday that it received formal written confirmation from Nasdaq, stating that the company has regained compliance with Nasdaq's minimum bid price requirement of $1 per share.
  • GAME -2.63% after hours to $1.11.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Online learning platform Coursera soars after turning a corner in Q2
data.description:
Coursera (NYSE:COUR) rallied to a 52-week high after the online learning company delivered a strong Q2 earnings report.

Revenue rose 10% year-over-year to $187.1 million and surpassed analyst expectations by nearly $6.6 million. Adjusted earnings per share of $0.12 was ahead of the consensus estimate of $0.09. The EPS mark was the best recorded by Coursera (NYSE:COUR) in the last three years. Gross profit grew to $102.7 million, up 14% from the prior year, and free cash flow rose to $29 million, a 68% jump.

The results were driven by robust growth across both consumer and enterprise segments, with the company attracting 7.5 million new learners and increasing its total registered learners to 183 million. Paid enterprise customers rose 12% to 1,686. As a result of this momentum, Coursera raised its full-year 2025 revenue forecast to a range of $738 million to $746 million, up from previous guidance and with a midpoint ahead of the consensus expectation.

Weighing in on the report, Morgan Stanley said the revenue and profit improvement marked another step in the right direction, although the firm kept a cautious tone

"Improving growth is encouraging and lowers likelihood of downside to estimates and shares, weakening the bear case, though it's likely too soon for investors to get onboard with a bull case," updated analyst Josh Baer.

Shares of Coursera (COUR) jumped 36.1% and swapped hands as high as $12.49 per share. That price is still far below the $36 IPO pricing level from March 2021 and the all-time closing high of $58.00 in April 2021.

data.title: OpenAI competitor Anthropic wants more funding to double valuation - report
data.description:
• Anthropic, an AI startup widely seen as a rival to OpenAI, is said to have had early talks for a new funding round that would more than double its valuation to more than $150B.
  • The startup is currently valued at $61.5B.
  • The next round would bring in at least $3B, but could potentially reach $5B, the Financial Times reported. 
  • The newspaper added that Anthropic has spoken with several Middle Eastern investors. MGX, an AI investment fund based in Abu Dhabi, has expressed interest.
  • Anthropic, which is known for its large language model Claude, has significant investment commitments from Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL). Open AI has major backing from Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT).
